summary: Drew DeVault argues that postmarketOS represents a revolutionary improvement over the chaotic XDA Forums era of smartphone hacking. He contrasts the old world of illegally closed-source Android forks, adfly links, and disorganized forum threads with postmarketOS's structured, community-driven approach built on Alpine Linux. While praising its proper version control, collaborative development model, and alignment with free software values, he acknowledges the difficult engineering challenges ahead. He concludes that postmarketOS paired with Pine64 hardware represents the best hope for delivering a truly free Linux smartphone platform.
key_points: The XDA Forums era was characterized by individual prestige-seeking, GPL violations, and poor collaboration — the antithesis of free software values
postmarketOS's use of Alpine Linux and proper development infrastructure (git repos, issue trackers, wikis) represents a fundamental shift from the Android hacking chaos
Android is Linux in implementation but antithetical to free software in spirit, laden with vendor lock-in, predatory apps, and poorly documented proprietary code
The path forward requires difficult engineering work, better cross-project cooperation, and narrower prioritization of key issues blocking a usable Linux smartphone
postmarketOS and Pine64 together represent the most promising foundation for smartphone freedom
money_quotes: Android may be Linux in implementation, but it's about as far from the spirit of free software as you can get.

Where we once had to live with illegally closed-source forks of the Linux kernel, we now have a git repository in which upstream Linux releases are tracked with a series of auditable patches for supporting various devices.
Where we once had a forum thread with five wrong answers to the same question on page 112, we now have a bug report on GitLab with a documented workaround and a merge request pending review.
pmOS does not seek to elevate the glories of tiny individual hackers clutching their secrets close to their chest, instead elevating the glory of the community as a whole.
Most smart phones are essentially badly put-together black boxes and most smart phone hackers are working with their legs cut off.
It will require difficult, boring engineering work, and will need the full attention and dedication of the talented people working on these projects.
key_insight: postmarketOS's greatest innovation isn't technical but cultural — replacing the chaotic, prestige-driven XDA hacking ecosystem with a collaborative, properly structured free software community that could finally deliver Linux on smartphones.
